title: Soluble or Insoluble? Will it settle?
activity: Topic M2.S4.T9 — Soluble vs insoluble substances in water (will it settle at the bottom or stay dissolved?)
description: A tap-and-select Class 6 tool that builds the soluble/insoluble distinction through a glass of water. Eight everyday substances — salt, sand, tea leaves, sugar, chalk powder, lemon juice, chalk pieces, mud — are dropped one at a time into a glass of water on screen. For each, after watching the substance fall in and the water swirl, the student PREDICTS one of two outcomes: 'settles at the bottom' (the substance is insoluble) or 'stays dissolved' (the substance is soluble). The glass then animates the real result — a soluble substance disappears and the water takes on a faint, uniform tint, while an insoluble substance swirls as a cloud and then settles as a band at the bottom. A green tick confirms a correct prediction; a wrong prediction shows a one-line hint stating whether that substance dissolves in water and why it does (or does not) settle. It is a SINGLE guided flow (no separate quiz mode): the student walks all eight substances with Back/Next (advance gated on answering), and after the last one a gamified summary scorecard shows the score (count-up, stars, confetti), a per-substance right/wrong recap, and a 'Start over' button. No drag-drop anywhere — the student only taps one of the two outcome buttons.
toolType: predict-then-reveal guided explorer (single flow, no quiz mode)
prodSafe: No 'lucide-react' import — all icons are inline SVGs defined in the file, so the tool renders on every iframe-renderer version (an external icon name can never be undefined at runtime).

gradeLevel.description: Tier preset. 6 = heaviest gamification, story framing, biggest text, slowest/most dramatic settle animation, 70-particle confetti + 5 stars on the summary. 7 = moderate. 8 = light/formal. Authored for Class 6.

themeColor.description: Accent hint. The tool uses the Singularity palette: header purple, water blue, sediment brown, CTAs orange #FF7212.

showNavigation.description: Show Back/Next between substances (advance is gated on answering each card).

autoPlayDuration.description: Host auto-advance hint, in ms. Ignored — the tool calls setStopAutoNext(true) because every card needs interaction.

substances.description: Which substance cards appear and in what order. Pass slugs from the built-in library (salt, sand, tea_leaves, sugar, chalk_powder, lemon_juice, chalk_pieces, mud), or full objects to add/override one: { slug, name, emoji, outcome: 'settles' | 'dissolves', color, solutionTint, hint }. 'settles' = insoluble; 'dissolves' = soluble. Use a subset to shorten the lesson or focus on specific substances.

showHints.description: Show the one-line dissolve/insoluble hint on a wrong answer (and the inline coaching captions).

feedbackMessage.description: Optional one-line coaching message (reserved; the tool already supplies per-card prompts).

builtInSubstances.name: Salt
builtInSubstances.hint: Salt dissolves in water — it spreads through and disappears, so it never settles.

builtInSubstances.name: Sand
builtInSubstances.hint: Sand is insoluble — it does not dissolve, so the heavier grains sink to the bottom.

builtInSubstances.name: Tea leaves
builtInSubstances.hint: Tea leaves are insoluble and heavier than water, so they settle at the bottom.

builtInSubstances.name: Sugar
builtInSubstances.hint: Sugar dissolves in water — it disappears into the liquid, so nothing settles.

builtInSubstances.name: Chalk powder
builtInSubstances.hint: Chalk does not dissolve — the fine powder is insoluble and slowly settles down.

builtInSubstances.name: Lemon juice
builtInSubstances.hint: Lemon juice mixes completely with water (it is soluble/miscible), so nothing settles.

builtInSubstances.name: Chalk pieces
builtInSubstances.hint: Chalk is insoluble — the solid pieces do not dissolve, so they sink to the bottom.

builtInSubstances.name: Mud
builtInSubstances.hint: Mud is insoluble — given time the heavier particles settle, leaving clearer water above.

steps.title: Predict each substance
steps.description: For each of the 8 substances: it drops into the glass and swirls; the student waits, then taps 'Settles at the bottom' or 'Stays dissolved'. Correct → green tick + confirmation; wrong → one-line hint on whether it dissolves. Back/Next move between substances (Next gated on answering).

steps.title: Summary scorecard
steps.description: After the last substance, a gamified summary: score count-up out of 8, 5 stars + confetti (≥60%), a per-substance right/wrong recap grid, tier encouragement, and a 'Start over' button.

interactionNotes: Tap/click only — two outcome buttons ('Settles at the bottom' / 'Stays dissolved'). No drag-and-drop of cards (house rule; robust on mobile for young students).

The glass is a single responsive SVG side-view scene driven by t (0..1 drop+settle progress), the substance's outcome, and a revealed flag. Soluble → the water takes a faint uniform tint and nothing settles; insoluble → particles swirl as a cloud and then settle as a band at the bottom.
Predict-then-reveal: the outcome buttons unlock only after the substance has been dropped in and watched; the real result is animated after the student commits a guess.
Single guided flow — there is NO separate quiz/practice mode and no mode selector. After all 8 substances the student reaches one summary scorecard (score + per-substance recap + Start over).
Answer key: salt, sugar and lemon juice are SOLUBLE (stay dissolved); sand, tea leaves, chalk powder, chalk pieces and mud are INSOLUBLE (settle at the bottom).
Calls setStopAutoNext(true) and reports setStepDetails on every card change.
Responsive from 320px; touch targets 56px at Class 6; the glass scene caps at 320px wide and scales down with the container.
PROD-SAFE ICONS: no lucide-react dependency — icons are inline SVGs, so no 'X is not defined' error regardless of the renderer's injected icon allowlist.
instructions_for_student: Drop each substance into the glass of water and wait. Decide whether it settles at the bottom (insoluble) or stays dissolved (soluble) — get a green tick when you're right!
